draft.introduction: Theophilus of Antioch’s Ad Autolycum (c. 180 CE) is an early Christian apology addressed to a pagan friend. It is the first surviving text to use the word Trias (Trinity) for the Godhead and contains the first Christian commentary on Genesis.

translation.text: If you say, “Show me your God,” I will say to you, “Show me your man, and I will show you my God.” Show me that the eyes of your soul see and the ears of your heart hear. For just as those who look with the eyes of the body perceive earthly life, so those who look with the eyes of the soul perceive God. God is seen by those who are able to see him, when the eyes of the soul are opened.
translation.original_text: εἰ λέγεις Δεῖξόν μοι τὸν θεόν σου

translator_notes.note: Theophilus’s argument—that the capacity to see God depends on the condition of the viewer—anticipates the patristic principle that “only the pure in heart see God” (Matthew 5:8). Knowledge of God is not merely intellectual but requires moral and spiritual transformation. This epistemological principle is foundational for the theosis tradition.
